reasoning: The SOFR Index is a cumulative measure that reflects the compounding of daily Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR) values. Because the US SOFR rate is currently positive (around 4.5%) and US interest rates have historically never gone below zero (nor do Fed policies support negative rates), the index structurally increases on every single business day. To fail to increase compared to the 2025-03-30 baseline, interest rates would either have to instantaneously drop to exactly zero today and stay there, or go deeply negative to erase all the accrued gains. Neither is a realistic monetary scenario. The only non-trivial tail risks are data discontinuation, structural formula changes, re-benchmarking the index back to 1.0 (highly unusual for active financial reference rates), or platform outages blocking publication. Because uncertainty dynamically compound with time — and per instructions to restrict predictions between 0.05 and 0.95 while making distant horizons proportionally nearer to 0.5 — the near-term probabilities are constrained at the 95% cap, graduating down cautiously to 85% for early 2026 to account for unforeseeable black-swan structural changes or unresolved index definitions out that far.
